title: Getting Ready for School
summary: A mum and her young daughter rush to get ready on a school morning — picking clothes, eating breakfast, and hunting for the missing school bag.

body.speaker: Mum
body.text: Ella, it's time to wake up! It's half past seven.

body.speaker: Mum
body.text: Come on, love. You don't want to be late. What do you want to wear today?

body.speaker: Child
body.text: Can I wear my red jumper? It's my favourite.

body.speaker: Mum
body.text: Of course! It's clean and on your chair. Now go and wash your face.

body.speaker: Mum
body.text: I made porridge. It will keep you warm. Your bowl is on the table.

body.speaker: Child
body.text: Can I have some honey in it, please?

body.speaker: Mum
body.text: Yes, it's on the shelf. Eat quickly though — the bus comes in fifteen minutes.

body.speaker: Child
body.text: Mum! I can't find my school bag!

body.speaker: Mum
body.text: Did you check behind the front door? That's where you left it yesterday.

body.speaker: Child
body.text: Oh! Yes, here it is. I found it!

body.speaker: Mum
body.text: Good. Coat on, and let's go. We don't want to miss the bus.

body.speaker: Child
body.text: Ready! Bye, house! Let's go, Mum!

annotations.exampleEn: The train leaves at half past seven, so don't be late.

annotations.meaningVi: áo len chui đầu (tiếng Anh Anh)
annotations.exampleEn: She put on a warm jumper because it was cold outside.
He has porridge with fruit every morning for breakfast.
She stirs a spoonful of honey into her tea.
The jars of jam are on the top shelf.
She packed her school bag the night before.
He left his umbrella beside the front door.
Run fast or we'll miss the bus!
Your shirt is clean — I washed it this morning.
Have some soup — it will keep you warm on a cold day.

annotations.meaningVi: nào, thôi nào (thúc giục ai đó)
annotations.exampleEn: 'Come on, we'll be late!' she called to her sister.

grammarNotes.title: Modal Verb 'Can' for Requests
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Can I...?' là cách hỏi xin phép hoặc đề nghị điều gì một cách thông dụng và thoải mái. Ví dụ: 'Can I wear my red jumper?' (Con có thể mặc áo len đỏ không?) và 'Can I have some honey?' (Con có thể lấy một ít mật ong không?). Đây là cách trẻ em thường dùng để xin phép bố mẹ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Can I...?' is used to ask permission informally. Example: 'Can I wear my red jumper?' / 'Can I have some honey?' This is the most common way children ask permission from parents in everyday speech. For more formal situations, 'May I...?' is used.

grammarNotes.title: Imperatives for Instructions and Encouragement
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu mệnh lệnh (imperative) dùng để ra lệnh, hướng dẫn, hoặc khuyến khích. Mum dùng nhiều câu mệnh lệnh: 'Come on!', 'Go and wash your face.', 'Eat quickly.', 'Coat on, and let's go.' Câu mệnh lệnh thường bắt đầu bằng động từ nguyên mẫu. 'Let's + verb' là câu đề nghị cùng làm gì đó.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Imperatives give instructions or encouragement. They start with the base verb: 'Come on!', 'Go and wash your face.', 'Eat quickly.', 'Coat on.' 'Let's + verb' is a suggestion to do something together: 'Let's go' means 'I suggest we go now.'

translationVi:
Mẹ: Ella, đến giờ dậy rồi! Bảy giờ rưỡi rồi đó.
Con: Đã rồi hả? Con buồn ngủ lắm, mẹ ơi.
Mẹ: Thôi nào, cưng. Con không muốn đi học muộn đâu. Hôm nay con muốn mặc gì?
Con: Con có thể mặc chiếc áo len đỏ không? Đó là cái con thích nhất.
Mẹ: Được chứ! Áo sạch rồi, để trên ghế của con đó. Giờ đi rửa mặt đi.
Con: Vâng ạ. Mẹ ơi, sáng nay ăn gì vậy?
Mẹ: Mẹ nấu cháo yến mạch rồi. Ăn vào sẽ ấm người. Bát của con để trên bàn đó.
Con: Con có thể cho thêm mật ong vào được không ạ?
Mẹ: Được, mật ong trên kệ đó. Nhưng ăn nhanh lên nhé — xe buýt đến trong mười lăm phút nữa thôi.
Con: Mẹ ơi! Con không tìm thấy cặp sách!
Mẹ: Con có tìm sau cửa trước chưa? Hôm qua con để ở đó đó.
Con: À! Có rồi, đây rồi. Con tìm thấy rồi!
Mẹ: Tốt rồi. Mặc áo khoác vào, rồi đi thôi. Đừng để lỡ xe buýt.
Con: Con sẵn sàng rồi! Tạm biệt ngôi nhà! Đi thôi mẹ ơi!

questions.prompt: What time does Mum wake Ella up?
questions.explanation: Mum says 'It's half past seven' — that is 7:30.
questions.hint: Listen to the exact time Mum says.

questions.prompt: What does Ella want to wear?
questions.explanation: Ella says 'Can I wear my red jumper? It's my favourite.'
questions.hint: What colour and type of clothing does Ella ask for?

questions.explanation: Mum says 'It's clean and on your chair.'
questions.hint: Where did Mum say the jumper was?

questions.prompt: What does Mum make for breakfast?
questions.hint: What warm breakfast food does Mum mention?

questions.prompt: Why does Mum say porridge is a good breakfast?
options: It is very quick to make
questions.explanation: Mum says 'It will keep you warm.'
questions.hint: What reason does Mum give for making porridge?

questions.prompt: What does Ella want to add to her porridge?
questions.explanation: Ella asks 'Can I have some honey in it, please?'
questions.hint: What sweet topping does Ella request?

questions.prompt: How long until the bus comes?
questions.explanation: Mum says 'the bus comes in fifteen minutes.'
questions.hint: How many minutes does Ella have left before the bus?

questions.prompt: Where was the missing school bag?
questions.explanation: Mum suggests 'Did you check behind the front door?' and Ella finds it there.
questions.hint: Where does Mum think Ella left it the day before?

questions.prompt: When did Ella leave the bag behind the door?
questions.explanation: Mum says 'That's where you left it yesterday.'
questions.hint: What time does Mum say Ella left the bag there?

questions.prompt: What does Mum remind Ella to put on before they leave?
questions.explanation: Mum says 'Coat on, and let's go.'
questions.hint: What item of clothing does Mum tell Ella to put on?

questions.prompt: What does Ella say as she leaves the house?
questions.explanation: Ella says 'Bye, house! Let's go, Mum!' — a playful farewell.
questions.hint: What are Ella's exact words as she heads out?

cloze.template:
Mum: Ella, it's time to wake up! It's half past {{b1}}.
Child: Already? I'm so tired, Mum.
Mum: Come on, love. You don't want to be late. What do you want to {{b2}} today?
Child: Can I wear my red {{b3}}? It's my favourite.
Mum: Of course! It's clean and on your chair. Now go and wash your face.
Child: Okay. Mum, what's for {{b4}}?
Mum: I made {{b5}}. It will keep you warm. Your bowl is on the table.
Child: Can I have some {{b6}} in it, please?
Mum: Yes, it's on the shelf. Eat quickly though — the bus comes in {{b7}} minutes.
Child: Mum! I can't find my school {{b8}}!
Mum: Did you check behind the front door? That's where you left it {{b9}}.
Child: Oh! Yes, here it is. I found it!
Mum: Good. Coat on, and let's go. We don't want to {{b10}} the bus.
Child: Ready! Bye, house! Let's go, Mum!

blanks.explanation: Mum says 'half past seven' — meaning 7:30 in the morning.
'What do you want to wear?' means what clothes does she want to put on.
A 'jumper' is a knitted pullover sweater (British English).
This is a morning scene, so Ella is asking about 'breakfast'.
'Porridge' is a warm oat-based breakfast — common in the UK, especially in winter.
Ella asks for 'honey' — a sweet liquid made by bees — to add flavour to her porridge.
Mum says 'the bus comes in fifteen minutes' — so they must hurry.
A 'school bag' is the bag children carry to school with their books and supplies.
Mum says 'That's where you left it yesterday' — the day before today.
'Miss the bus' means to arrive too late and not be able to get on it.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Ella nearly missed the bus because she couldn't find her school bag. Do you think it is a good idea to prepare your school bag the night before? What habits could help children like Ella have a less stressful morning?
